claim: Hand A's character-level statistical signature is closer to Esperanto (constructed-language baseline) than to natural agglutinative languages (Basque, Hungarian, Turkish) on at least 4 of 6 metrics, supporting the hypothesis that the M5 = 1.00 selectional rigidity (validated as genuine in H-BV-M5-RIGIDITY-DIAGNOSIS-01) reflects a constructed / artificial-system design rather than a natural-language pattern.
evidence_for: H-BV-M5-RIGIDITY-DIAGNOSIS-01 STRONG_GENUINE (3.0/3) validated that Hand A's 1.00 slot-selection strictness is non-random, non-partition-selected, and exceeds UD_Basque's 0.00 under Brain-V's own methodology. The rigidity is real but does not match natural agglutinative language patterns. Two parsimonious explanations remain: (a) Hand A encodes a constructed/artificial system where designer-imposed selectional rules produce the over-rigidity; (b) Hand A's glyphs correspond to morpheme classes (category tokens) rather than individual morphemes, which naturally produces stricter combinatorics. This hypothesis tests (a).

Constructed languages typically exhibit character-level signatures distinct from natural languages: lower conditional entropy (more predictable), lower Markov-reachability (fewer bigrams in active use), steeper or shallower Zipf slopes depending on design philosophy, and higher type-token ratios at fixed windows (less repetition). Esperanto is the best-studied constructed language with large corpora; Hungarian and Turkish are well-studied natural agglutinatives with UD treebanks. The comparison has established baselines on both sides.

Hand A's Zipf slope of ~0.75 (from H-BV-BASQUE-FREQ-DIST-01) is already known to be shallower than natural-language norms (~1.0). This is one data point suggesting constructed-system compatibility. The full 6-metric profile extends that single data point to a systematic comparison.

Reference corpora have all been obtained: UD_Basque-BDT (already fetched), UD_Hungarian-Szeged (1.4 MB), UD_Turkish-IMST (3.1 MB), Esperanto Wikipedia sample (362 KB plaintext). Random baselines (shuffled Hand A, Markov-1 generated) are computable from Hand A alone.
evidence_against: Character-level statistics depend on alphabet size. Hand A uses an EVA transliteration with ~20 distinct characters; Hungarian uses 44; Esperanto uses 28. Raw Shannon entropy is bounded above by log2(alphabet_size), so naive entropy comparison would systematically place small-alphabet corpora lower. Normalisation (entropy / max_entropy) is required; the comparison is then less direct.

Esperanto is ONE constructed language. Its statistical signature is specific to Zamenhof's design philosophy (Romance-Slavic roots, agglutinative affixation, regular grammar). A different constructed language (Lojban with predicate-logic grammar; Toki Pona with ~120 words; a priori philosophical languages) would show very different character-level statistics. Using Esperanto as the single constructed baseline may not represent 'constructed languages' as a class.

Hand A's character-level signature reflects EVA transliteration choices — the transliterator (Currier, Takahashi, Landini et al.) chose how to map glyphs to Latin letters. Statistics computed at the EVA level may reflect those choices as much as the underlying manuscript. A different transliteration (e.g., Currier's original) might produce different entropy values. The comparison is only valid under the locked ZL3b EVA transliteration.

The 4-of-6 threshold is arbitrary. If Hand A is closer to Esperanto on exactly 3 metrics and exactly 3 to natural agglutinatives, the PARTIAL verdict triggers — but this could reflect Hand A having genuinely mixed features, not a cleanly diagnosable category.

A confirmation here identifies a SIGNATURE match, not a DECIPHERMENT. Hand A might share statistical signatures with Esperanto without being Esperanto or any known constructed language. The result narrows candidate classes; it does not identify the source.
test:
LOCKED PROCEDURE.

  CORPORA (all plaintext extractions; character-level statistics computed on concatenated corpus per source):
    Hand A: tokens from voynich-corpus.json filtered to currier_language=='A'; concatenated with space separators after standard ligature-aware tokenisation preserving glyph boundaries. Use RAW character sequence for entropy metrics (not tokenised glyph units).
    Basque: UD_Basque-BDT FORM column extracted from raw/corpus/reference-corpora/eu_bdt-ud-train.conllu; concatenated with spaces.
    Hungarian: UD_Hungarian-Szeged FORM column from raw/corpus/reference-corpora/hu_szeged-ud-train.conllu; concatenated with spaces.
    Turkish: UD_Turkish-IMST FORM column from raw/corpus/reference-corpora/tr_imst-ud-train.conllu; concatenated with spaces.
    Esperanto: raw/corpus/reference-corpora/esperanto-sample.txt verbatim (lines prefixed with '===' filtered out as headers; blank lines filtered).
    Shuffled Hand A: same character sequence as Hand A but with each character position randomly re-sampled with replacement from the Hand A character distribution (random seed 42).
    Markov-1 Hand A: generated by sampling characters one at a time from the bigram distribution of Hand A (random seed 43); total length matched to Hand A.

  PREPROCESSING (LOCKED):
    All corpora normalised to lowercase before character-level statistics.
    Whitespace runs collapsed to single space.
    Punctuation and digits preserved (natural language signature includes them).
    Non-alphabetic non-standard marks (tags, etc.) removed if present.

  SIX METRICS (LOCKED):

    M1 — CHARACTER-LEVEL SHANNON ENTROPY (normalised):
      H_raw = -sum over characters c of p(c) * log2(p(c))
      alphabet_size = |distinct characters|
      H_max = log2(alphabet_size)
      H_normalised = H_raw / H_max  (bounded in [0, 1])
      REPORT: raw and normalised per corpus. Compare on NORMALISED.

    M2 — CONDITIONAL ENTROPY H(X_n | X_{n-1}):
      For each bigram (c_{n-1}, c_n), count p(c_{n-1}, c_n).
      H(X_n | X_{n-1}) = -sum over bigrams (a, b) of p(a, b) * log2(p(b | a))
      REPORT: raw per corpus. Natural language has moderate conditional entropy; constructed languages often lower (more predictable).

    M3 — MARKOV-MODEL REACHABILITY:
      bigrams_observed = count of distinct (c_{n-1}, c_n) pairs in the corpus
      alphabet_size = |distinct characters|
      bigrams_possible = alphabet_size^2
      reachability = bigrams_observed / bigrams_possible  (bounded in [0, 1])
      REPORT: per corpus. Lower values indicate more restricted combinatorics.

    M4 — ZIPF SLOPE:
      Tokenise each corpus into words (split on whitespace).
      Count word frequencies; rank descending.
      Fit log(rank) vs log(frequency) on the top-1000 words (or all if fewer).
      slope = |slope of linear fit|  (natural language ~1.0; shallower = more uniform)
      REPORT: per corpus. Hand A is expected near 0.75 from prior work.

    M5 — TYPE-TOKEN RATIO AT FIXED WINDOW:
      Take first N=5000 word tokens (or all if corpus has fewer).
      TTR = |distinct types| / |total tokens|
      REPORT: per corpus. Higher TTR = more vocabulary richness at the window.

    M6 — HEAPS' LAW SLOPE:
      For token positions n = 1, 2, 5, 10, 100, 500, 1000, 2000, 5000, 10000 (or max available):
      V(n) = distinct types in first n tokens
      Fit log(V) vs log(n) — slope is the Heaps' beta parameter.
      REPORT: per corpus. Natural languages typically have beta in [0.4, 0.6]. Constructed or artificial systems can have different beta.

  COMPARISON AND DECISION:

    After computing 6 metric values for each of 7 corpora (Hand A + 3 natural + Esperanto + 2 random baselines), for each metric:
      natural_mean = mean of (Basque, Hungarian, Turkish) values
      constructed = Esperanto value
      random_mean = mean of (Shuffled Hand A, Markov-1 Hand A) values
      hand_a_value = Hand A's measured value
      distance_to_natural = |hand_a_value - natural_mean|
      distance_to_constructed = |hand_a_value - constructed|
      distance_to_random = |hand_a_value - random_mean|
      closest_baseline = category with minimum distance (natural / constructed / random)

    Count how many metrics have Hand A closest to each category.

  DECISION RULE (LOCKED):
    CONFIRMED_CONSTRUCTED: Hand A closest to constructed on >= 4 of 6 metrics.
    REFUTED_CONSTRUCTED: Hand A closest to natural on >= 4 of 6 metrics. Explanation requires alternatives (glyph=morpheme-class candidate).
    CLOSEST_TO_RANDOM: Hand A closest to random on >= 4 of 6 metrics. Concerning: indicates the structural appearance may be measurement artefact; further investigation needed.
    PARTIAL: 3-3-3 distribution (no clear winner). Hand A has mixed features.
    NEAR_CONSTRUCTED: 3 closest to constructed + 2 or 3 close seconds — reports as informative but not decisive.

  WORKED EXAMPLE (for M1 normalised entropy):
    Hand A has ~20 distinct characters, alphabet_size = 20, H_max = log2(20) ~= 4.32. Suppose H_raw = 3.2. Then H_normalised = 3.2 / 4.32 ~= 0.74.
    Basque has ~27 distinct characters, H_max ~= 4.75. Suppose H_raw = 4.0. H_norm = 0.84.
    If Esperanto has H_norm = 0.78, Hand A (0.74) is closer to Esperanto (|0.74 - 0.78| = 0.04) than to Basque-et-al natural mean (say 0.85, distance 0.11).
test_metric: 6 metrics per corpus. For each metric, Hand A is assigned to natural / constructed / random based on minimum absolute distance.
test_threshold: CONFIRMED_CONSTRUCTED if 4+ metrics closest to Esperanto. REFUTED_CONSTRUCTED if 4+ closest to natural. CLOSEST_TO_RANDOM if 4+ closest to random. PARTIAL otherwise (3-3 or 3-2-1). NEAR_CONSTRUCTED annotation if 3 closest to constructed with others-close.
tests_remaining: locked_constructed_signature_test: parse all 7 corpora; compute 6 metrics each; rank Hand A's closeness to natural/constructed/random per metric; apply decision rule
reasoning: H-BV-M5-RIGIDITY-DIAGNOSIS-01 validated Hand A's M5 = 1.00 selectional rigidity as a real structural feature (not artefact) and showed it exceeds UD_Basque's 0.00 under Brain-V's methodology. The question then becomes: what kind of system produces this rigidity? A natural language with over-strict morphology has never been attested at this level; the more parsimonious hypotheses are constructed system or glyph=morpheme-class. This hypothesis tests the constructed-system hypothesis via character-level statistical comparison. Esperanto is the primary constructed-language baseline (well-documented, 362KB plaintext obtained). Basque + Hungarian + Turkish provide the natural-agglutinative baseline (all UD treebanks fetched). Random baselines (shuffled + Markov-1) provide a ceiling/floor reference. The six metrics collectively characterise a corpus's statistical signature at the character and word level — they're standard NLP diagnostics used in stylometry and authorship attribution. Methodology lesson 3 (external baseline reproduction) is partially applicable: the Esperanto sample is from Wikipedia, so it's a specific register; Hungarian/Turkish are from UD treebanks; all are reproducible. Caveat: Esperanto is one constructed language among many possible designs; confirmation here identifies a signature match not a decipherment.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-17 user-directed test following H-BV-M5-RIGIDITY-DIAGNOSIS-01 STRONG_GENUINE. Tests whether the genuine rigidity finding is explained by Hand A being a constructed/artificial system. Reference corpora fetched 2026-04-17 (UD_Hungarian-Szeged 1.4MB, UD_Turkish-IMST 3.1MB, Esperanto Wikipedia 362KB).
